toLevel5.question: Evaluate the argument that 'ethics has no factual content so evidence cannot support or change ethical views'.
toLevel5.correct: The argument overlooks factual claims embedded in ethical views about harms, capacities and consequences, which evidence can inform or correct
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Many ethical views contain factual claims open to evidential correction.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because no ethical view contains any factual claim anywhere and ethics is entirely disconnected from the empirical world in every case
distractors.explanation: Ethical views often rest on factual claims; evidence is relevant.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because factual claims are always irrelevant to ethical conclusions regardless of the domain or context in which the ethical question arises
distractors.explanation: Factual claims often matter for ethical conclusions.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because ethics has been redefined in contemporary philosophy as purely emotional expression with no factual component whatsoever
distractors.explanation: Contemporary ethics includes views where factual claims play substantive roles.

toLevel5.question: Evaluate the claim that 'cultural diversity weakens ethics by producing relativism that undermines all standards'.
toLevel5.correct: The claim overstates relativism; diversity coexists with shared frameworks, and negotiated standards can sustain ethical life across differences
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Shared frameworks support standards alongside diversity; relativism does not follow from diversity.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because diversity automatically produces relativism and relativism undermines all ethical standards in every society that becomes diverse
distractors.explanation: Diversity does not automatically produce relativism or undermine standards.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because ethical standards cannot operate across cultural difference in any form at any time in any society throughout recorded history
distractors.explanation: Standards operate across difference through shared frameworks.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because diverse societies have collapsed historically whenever they encountered more than one ethical tradition across their populations
distractors.explanation: Diverse societies endure with shared frameworks; collapse is not the general pattern.

toLevel5.question: Evaluate the argument that 'historical ethical change demonstrates that any view can be changed through pressure alone'.
toLevel5.correct: The argument conflates considered change with pressure-driven change; historical change typically combined pressure with dialogue, evidence and argument
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Historical change combined multiple supports; pressure alone is a partial description.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because all historical change has occurred through pressure alone with no role for dialogue or evidence anywhere in the process
distractors.explanation: Multiple supports operated alongside pressure in historical change.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because considered change has never occurred in any society and only pressure has produced any shift in any view ever
distractors.explanation: Considered change through dialogue and evidence is documented.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because pressure and considered change are identical concepts with identical outcomes regardless of the process used to produce change
distractors.explanation: The concepts differ; processes and legitimacy differ.

toLevel5.question: Evaluate the claim that 'human rights are a Western imposition with no legitimate claim to universal application'.
toLevel5.correct: The claim has force in some respects but overlooks contributions from many traditions to the framework and the overlapping consensus many societies have developed around it
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Human rights frameworks draw on many traditions and have developed through overlapping consensus globally.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because no non-Western tradition has contributed any element to human rights frameworks across any period of their development historically
distractors.explanation: Many traditions have contributed to human rights frameworks historically and contemporarily.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because human rights have no legitimacy outside the specific countries where they were first articulated formally in written documents
distractors.explanation: Legitimacy operates through overlapping consensus beyond specific countries.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because human rights frameworks have been rejected by every non-Western society in every period of contemporary international relations
distractors.explanation: Non-Western societies have engaged with and contributed to human rights frameworks.

toLevel5.question: Evaluate the argument that 'dialogue is a waste of time because people never change their minds'.
toLevel5.correct: The argument overlooks documented attitude change on many issues where dialogue contributed, including historical cases of gradual revision over decades
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Attitude change through dialogue is documented; the argument overstates minds' fixity.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because attitudes have never changed on any matter in any society at any time in recorded history regardless of dialogue occurring
distractors.explanation: Attitudes change historically; dialogue has contributed.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because dialogue has been banned in contemporary societies as a matter of public policy across most jurisdictions surveyed
distractors.explanation: Dialogue operates broadly and is not banned.

distractors.answer: The argument is fully correct because people are biologically incapable of changing views after age twenty and dialogue therefore affects nobody
distractors.explanation: Views change throughout the lifespan through exposure and reflection.

toLevel5.question: Evaluate the claim that 'negotiated shared ethical perspectives are inferior to imposed ones because negotiation produces weaker commitments'.
toLevel5.correct: The claim misreads legitimacy and durability; negotiated commitments typically sustain broader support and survive disagreement better than imposed uniform views
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Negotiated commitments often have stronger legitimacy and durability than imposed ones.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because imposed views always produce stronger commitments than negotiated ones regardless of the context or population involved in any case
distractors.explanation: Imposed views often lack the durability of negotiated commitments.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because negotiation always weakens commitments and imposition always strengthens them across all contemporary diverse societies studied
distractors.explanation: Negotiation often strengthens commitments through legitimacy.

distractors.answer: The claim is fully correct because imposed uniform views have been shown to survive all disagreement while negotiated ones have collapsed under any challenge encountered
distractors.explanation: Negotiated views often survive disagreement better; the claim inverts the pattern.
